title: 1553rd meeting (9-11 March 2026) (DH) - H46-19 Sidiropoulos and Papakostas and Alkhatib and Others groups v. Greece (Applications Nos. 33349/10, 3566/16)
text: 3 CM/Del/Dec(2026)1553/H46-19 MINISTERS’ DEPUTIES Decisions CM/Del/Dec(2026)1553/H46-19 11 March 2026 1553rd meeting, 9-11 March 2026 (DH) H46-19 Sidiropoulos and Papakostas and Alkhatib and Others groups v. Greece (Applications Nos. 33349/10, 3566/16) Supervision of the execution of the European Court’s judgments Reference document CM/Notes/1553/H46-19 Decisions The Deputies 1. recalling that the Sidiropoulos and Papakostas group of cases concern ill-treatment by law enforcement agents and the lack of effective investigations in this regard, including into discriminatory attitudes by the law enforcement agents, while the Alkhatib and Others group concerns substantive violations of right to life in the context of the coastguard interception operations and ineffective investigations into the death and serious injuries that occurred during these operations; As regards individual measures 2. noted with regret that no further measures are possible in Kostantinopoulos No. 2 and Torosian, where the competent independent authorities have assessed the possibility of reopening of the impugned investigations and concluded that no further investigatory steps can be taken for practical or legal reasons; decided therefore, without prejudice to the assessment of the outstanding general measures which will continue to be supervised in the Sidiropoulos and Papakostas group of cases, to close these cases by adopting Final Resolution CM/ResDH(2026)30; 3. invited the authorities to provide further information regarding the status of the criminal and/or administrative investigations in the remaining cases of these groups, as set out in the Secretariat’s analysis; As regards general measures Sidiropoulos and Papakostas group - substantive violations of Article 3 4. noted with interest the authorities’ continued efforts to prevent similar violations through professional training of law enforcement officers demonstrating their sustained efforts to prevent similar violations and strongly encouraged them to continue those activities; 5. noting, however, that those efforts have not yet comprehensively resolved the long-standing and complex problem of ill-treatment by law-enforcement agents, as serious concerns continue to be raised in this regard, including by the Greek Ombudsman; strongly encouraged the authorities to submit information on the implementation of procedural safeguards, as recommended by the European Committee for the Prevention of Torture and Inhuman or Degrading Treatment or Punishment (CPT), in order to prevent similar substantive violations of Article 3; and requested, in particular, information regarding access to a doctor and the practical application of procedural safeguards to suspects from the outset of deprivation of liberty; Sidiropoulos and Papakostas group - procedural violations of Article 3 and violation of Article 14 in conjunction with Article 3 6. recalling that lack of thoroughness of criminal investigations into allegations of ill-treatment has been identified by the Court as a cross-cutting issue in several judgments pending before the Committee of Ministers, expressed their regret about the lack of progress in the conduct of targeted wide scale training activities for prosecutors and judges, which remain necessary; urged the authorities to explore all possible avenues of further action to ensure that criminal investigations are sufficiently thorough and conducted in a fully Convention-compliant manner, including through large scale trainings and awareness raising of prosecutors and judges; underlining that the Secretariat remains at the authorities’ disposal to assist, strongly encouraged the authorities to make full use of Council of Europe expertise in this domain; 7. recalling that the Mechanism for the Investigation of Arbitrary Incidents, established in response to the Court’s judgments, has been operational for eight years and has substantially contributed to enhancing the quality of disciplinary investigations, noted with regret that certain previously identified shortcomings in these investigations persist, including the limited use of Sworn Administrative Investigations instead of simple inquiries; strongly invited, therefore, the authorities to provide information on the implementation of the outstanding recommendations issued by the Mechanism; 8. invited the authorities to submit their evaluation of the general measures necessary in response to the Panayotopoulos and Others judgment concerning also the failure to investigate discriminatory motives on the part of the law enforcement agents; Alkhatib and Others group - substantive violations of Article 2 9. welcoming the authorities’ readiness to review the legislative framework governing the use of firearms by the Coastguard and to pursue further discussions with the Secretariat to that end, strongly encouraged them to consider abolishing or amending the 1992 Rules of Engagement, to align them with the 2004 Regulations, which were found prima facie adequate by the Court in Alkhatib and Others; 10. noting further with interest the authorities’ commitment to integrate the Court’s findings into sea border control practices, as well as the training already provided to the Coastguard, invited the authorities to sustain these efforts and extend such training beyond cadet officers to all coastguard officers, with a particular focus on the conduct of interception operations in line with the Court’s standards; 11. strongly invited the authorities also to review and, if necessary, amend the legislative and administrative framework governing interception operations, and to ensure their effective implementation in light of the shortcomings identified by the Court in this group, notably by ensuring that such operations are conducted within a sufficiently clear and regulated framework, incorporating adequate and effective safeguards for human life and treating the protection of the right to life as an absolute priority; further invited them to consider the effective use of cameras in all vessels engaging in interception operations; Alkhatib and Others group - procedural violations of Article 2 12. stressed as set out above the need for full engagement by the authorities in a large-scale training and awareness-raising programme for prosecutors and judges, which in the context of this group should in particular include naval courts and prosecutors, given their specific competence; invited the authorities to submit their evaluation of the measures required in response to the Court’s specific findings in Almukhlas and Al-Maliki concerning the lack of independence of investigations; 13. noting with interest that the authorities are currently reflecting on the establishment of an independent border monitoring mechanism, invited them to ensure that any such mechanism, once established, is provided with the mandate to oversee border control activities and interception operations and equipped with the necessary competencies and resources to effectively address the shortcomings identified by the Court in these judgments and to support criminal investigations into similar incidents; 14. decided to resume their examination of these groups of cases at one of their Human Rights meetings in 2027.
